content.text: Amazon Bedrock is a fully managed AWS service that hosts foundation models — including Claude — inside your AWS account's network boundary. Instead of calling
using your existing AWS IAM credentials. Bedrock handles provisioning, scaling, and reliability; you pay per token through your AWS bill.

The primary reasons to choose Bedrock over the Direct API are: data residency (your request and response stay in your chosen AWS region), compliance boundary alignment with AWS's existing certifications (HIPAA, SOC 2, PCI DSS), integration with the broader AWS ecosystem (VPC, CloudWatch, Lambda, S3), and consolidated billing with existing AWS spend.

your organization has an AWS-first policy, needs a data residency commitment in writing, must satisfy HIPAA or FedRAMP requirements, or wants to colocate Claude next to other AWS services like SageMaker, Lambda, or Aurora.

attrs.alt: Amazon Bedrock integration architecture showing Your Application, Bedrock, Claude Model, IAM, VPC Endpoint, and key features

content.text: Bedrock uses AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) — not API keys — for authentication. Your application assumes an IAM role or uses IAM user credentials. You must grant the role permission to call Bedrock inference actions on the specific model ARN.
to the exact model ARNs your application uses rather than
. This limits the blast radius if the role is compromised. Attach the policy to a task role (ECS/Lambda) or EC2 instance profile rather than long-lived IAM user credentials.
Setting Up Credentials for Local Development

# Install AWS CLI and configure a profile
pip install awscli boto3 anthropic[bedrock]
aws configure --profile my-claude-dev
# Prompts for: Access Key ID, Secret Access Key, Region (us-east-1), Output format

# Or use environment variables (preferred for CI)
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIA...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
export AWS_DEFAULT_REGION=us-east-1
The Bedrock runtime API uses a generic
call — the model-specific request format is passed as a JSON body. For Claude, that body uses the same
structure as the Direct API, but wrapped in Bedrock's envelope.

import anthropic

# AnthropicBedrock client handles boto3 auth transparently
client = anthropic.AnthropicBedrock(
    aws_region="us-east-1",          # or set AWS_DEFAULT_REGION
    # aws_profile="my-claude-dev",   # optional; uses default profile if omitted
)

message = client.messages.create(
    model="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",       # Bedrock model ID (prefix anthropic.)
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Explain IAM roles in 2 sentences."}],
)
print(message.content[0].text)

import boto3, json

bedrock = boto3.client("bedrock-runtime", region_name="us-east-1")

body = {
    "anthropic_version": "bedrock-2023-05-31",
    "max_tokens": 1024,
    "messages": [{"role": "user", "content": "Explain IAM roles in 2 sentences."}],
}

response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",
    body=json.dumps(body),
    contentType="application/json",
    accept="application/json",
)

result = json.loads(response["body"].read())
print(result["content"][0]["text"])
client is simpler because it handles the Bedrock envelope, version header, and response parsing automatically. The
approach is useful if you need direct access to raw Bedrock response metadata (e.g.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicBedrock(aws_region="us-east-1")

with client.messages.stream(
    model="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Write a short story about a robot."}],
) as stream:
    for text in stream.text_stream:
        print(text, end="", flush=True)
print()   # newline after stream
context manager handles chunked SSE parsing identically to the Direct API, so the same streaming code works with both clients with only the client constructor changing.

Cross-region inference profiles let Bedrock automatically route your request to the healthiest region within a geography when your home region is throttled or degraded. You opt in by using a cross-region model ID instead of the base model ID.

# Cross-region inference: route within US automatically
response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="us.anthropic.claude-opus-4-8-20251101-v1:0",   # full versioned ARN
    body=json.dumps(body),
    contentType="application/json",
    accept="application/json",
)
cross geography boundaries. Data declared as EU stays in the EU geography even when routed from eu-west-1 to eu-central-1. This maintains GDPR data residency while increasing throughput.

Bedrock Batch Inference processes large jobs asynchronously at 50% of the on-demand price. You write JSONL input to S3, create a batch job, and read JSONL output from S3 when complete. Batch jobs process up to 50,000 records per job and typically complete within 24 hours.
The input JSONL format uses the same
body as the InvokeModel API, one object per line, each with a
for correlation. The output JSONL includes
, and error info for failed records.
Bedrock Guardrails is a managed content filtering layer you can attach to any Bedrock model invocation. You create a guardrail in the console or via API, then pass its ID in your
call. Bedrock applies the filter both to the input and output before returning the response.

response = bedrock.invoke_model(
    modelId="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",
    body=json.dumps(body),
    contentType="application/json",
    accept="application/json",
    guardrailIdentifier="abc123xyz",     # from create_guardrail response
    guardrailVersion="DRAFT",
)

result = json.loads(response["body"].read())
# result may include "amazon-bedrock-guardrailAction": "INTERVENED"
if result.get("amazon-bedrock-guardrailAction") == "INTERVENED":
    print("Guardrail blocked or altered the response")
    print(result.get("amazon-bedrock-guardrailCoverage"))
else:
    print(result["content"][0]["text"])

Guardrails support: topic denial (deny finance advice), content filters (hate, violence, sexual), PII redaction (auto-redact SSN, credit card), and word filters (custom blocked terms). They operate at the Bedrock layer, not inside Claude, so they add latency (~50–150 ms) but work consistently across model versions.
Bedrock automatically publishes metrics to CloudWatch under the
namespace. These are per-region, per-model metrics with no additional setup.

in the Bedrock console to capture full request/response payloads in CloudWatch Logs or S3. This is essential for debugging and compliance auditing, but note that logging prompt content means that data lands in your CloudWatch log group — ensure retention policies match your data classification requirements.
By default, Bedrock calls exit your VPC and traverse the public internet. A

(PrivateLink) for Bedrock routes traffic entirely within the AWS network — no NAT gateway, no internet gateway, no public IP required. This is required for workloads that must not touch the public internet.

# Create VPC endpoint for Bedrock runtime
aws ec2 create-vpc-endpoint   --vpc-id vpc-0abc1234   --service-name com.amazonaws.us-east-1.bedrock-runtime   --vpc-endpoint-type Interface   --subnet-ids subnet-0def5678   --security-group-ids sg-0ghi9012   --private-dns-enabled
Once the endpoint is active and private DNS is enabled, all SDK calls to
from within the VPC will resolve to private IP addresses automatically — no code change needed.
Prompt Caching reduces input token cost and latency for requests that share a large, static prefix (system prompts, documents, few-shot examples). When you mark a content block with
, Bedrock caches the KV representations up to that point and reuses them on cache-hit requests.

import anthropic

client = anthropic.AnthropicBedrock(aws_region="us-east-1")

LARGE_CONTEXT = open("reference_doc.txt").read()   # e.g. 20,000 tokens

message = client.messages.create(
    model="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",
    max_tokens=1024,
    system=[
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": LARGE_CONTEXT,
            "cache_control": {"type": "ephemeral"},
        },
        {
            "type": "text",
            "text": "Answer user questions based only on the document above.",
        },
    ],
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "What is the refund policy?"}],
)

# Inspect cache usage in usage block
usage = message.usage
print(f"Cache write tokens: {getattr(usage, 'cache_creation_input_tokens', 0)}")
print(f"Cache read tokens:  {getattr(usage, 'cache_read_input_tokens', 0)}")
print(f"Input tokens:       {usage.input_tokens}")
First request; cache not yet warm
of up to 1 hour (vs 5 minutes on the Direct API) via a separate
cache tier. Longer TTL is useful for batch processing workloads where requests arrive over tens of minutes.
One of Bedrock's advantages is colocation with the AWS ecosystem. Common integration patterns:
Grant the Lambda function's execution role
permission. Lambda's execution environment is in the same AWS network as Bedrock, so calls are fast and never touch the public internet.

A common pattern: ingest raw documents to S3 → Lambda generates JSONL → Bedrock Batch processes overnight → Lambda reads output JSONL → writes results to DynamoDB. The whole pipeline costs roughly 50% of on-demand pricing for the inference step.
Bedrock with Amazon Bedrock Knowledge Bases (RAG)
Bedrock Knowledge Bases is a managed RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) service. You connect an S3 data source, Bedrock indexes it using embeddings, and you query it with

Bedrock imposes throughput quotas per model per region. Unlike the Direct API (which has a single quota pool), Bedrock quotas are shared across your AWS account within a region. Default quotas are conservative; you can request increases via the AWS Service Quotas console.
When you hit a quota, Bedrock returns HTTP 429 (ThrottlingException). The
do not auto-retry on 429 by default. Implement explicit retry logic with exponential backoff, or use the cross-region inference profile to spread load across regions automatically.
Migrating from the Direct API to Bedrock
If you already use the Direct API, migrating to Bedrock via the Anthropic SDK requires only changing the client constructor — all other code stays the same:

# Before (Direct API)
import anthropic
client = anthropic.Anthropic(api_key="sk-ant-...")

# After (Bedrock) — same .messages.create() interface
import anthropic
client = anthropic.AnthropicBedrock(aws_region="us-east-1")

# Model IDs: add "anthropic." prefix and remove dashes in version dates
# Direct API:  "claude-opus-4-8"
# Bedrock:     "anthropic.claude-opus-4-8"

message = client.messages.create(
    model="anthropic.claude-opus-4-8",   # Bedrock model ID
    max_tokens=1024,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": "Hello"}],
)
works the same on both clients

Amazon Bedrock hosts Claude inside your AWS account boundary, making it the right choice when data residency, HIPAA compliance, or AWS ecosystem integration matter. Authentication uses IAM roles rather than API keys. The

client mirrors the Direct API interface, minimizing migration effort. Key features — cross-region inference, batch inference at 50% cost, Guardrails for content filtering, and native CloudWatch metrics — are not available on the Direct API. For private networking, add a VPC Interface Endpoint to keep traffic off the public internet.
